text: Vietnamese Text-to-Speech System Using Artificial Intelligence (AI)

Abstract. Text-to-Speech (TTS) technology plays a vital role in modern applications such as virtual assistants, assistive systems for visually impaired users, and human–machine interaction platforms. For Vietnamese, developing TTS systems remains challenging due to the language’s monosyllabic structure with a rich tonal system, as well as issues in text processing and normalization. This paper presents a systematic survey of Vietnamese TTS approaches based on Artificial Intelligence (AI), covering the evolution from traditional statistical models to modern deep learning architectures and end-to-end frameworks. The research methodology involves collecting, classifying, and analyzing representative domestic and international studies related to Vietnamese speech synthesis. The findings indicate that speech quality and naturalness have significantly improved with advanced neural network models; however, challenges remain in tonal modeling, voice adaptation, and data requirements. Based on these findings, the paper identifies current research gaps and proposes potential future directions for Vietnamese TTS systems.
Keywords: Text-to-Speech (TTS); Vietnamese speech synthesis; Artificial Intelligence (AI); Deep Learning; Neural TTS; End-to-End TTS; Zero-Shot TTS.
Introduction

In recent years, the rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has significantly accelerated progress in the field of speech processing, in which Text-to-Speech (TTS) technology plays a crucial role in various applications such as virtual assistants, education, customer service, and assistive systems for visually impaired users [6]. TTS enhances user experience and expands human–computer interaction capabilities through the generation of natural-sounding speech.

In Vietnam, alongside digital transformation and the growth of online services, the demand for Vietnamese TTS applications has been increasing steadily. Several domestic technology enterprises and research organizations, including Vbee, Viettel, VinAI, FPT, and Zalo AI, have invested in the research and deployment of TTS systems to address diverse practical needs [19, 25]. Beyond its economic value, TTS also holds significant social importance in supporting visually impaired individuals and promoting smart education models [3, 8, 22].

Despite substantial progress, Vietnamese TTS still faces numerous challenges due to the language’s monosyllabic characteristics and complex tonal system, which require highly accurate modeling of pitch, duration, and intonation [12, 32]. In addition, issues related to input text processing and normalization have not been fully resolved, directly affecting the quality of synthesized speech.

The emergence of modern approaches such as end-to-end TTS, multi-speaker TTS, and zero-shot TTS has introduced new research directions, significantly improving naturalness and system flexibility. However, comprehensive survey studies on Vietnamese TTS remain limited, often lacking clear classification and systematic comparison among model groups across different development stages [13, 27, 40].

Motivated by these challenges, this survey aims to systematically review and analyze Vietnamese TTS approaches, ranging from traditional methods to modern deep learning architectures, in order to clarify their strengths, limitations, and future development trends. The paper is logically organized, including background knowledge, analysis of representative model groups, comparison of research outcomes, discussion of existing challenges, and suggestions for future research directions.
Theoretical Background and Related Technologies

A Text-to-Speech (TTS) system consists of a sequence of processing components designed to convert input text into audible and natural-sounding speech signals. In general, a modern TTS system comprises three main modules: text processing, an acoustic model, and a waveform generator (vocoder). The rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence, particularly deep learning, has enabled TTS systems to evolve from rule-based and traditional statistical approaches to end-to-end models with significantly improved speech quality [16, 17, 23].
Text Processing in Vietnamese TTS Systems

Text processing is the first and critically important stage in a Vietnamese TTS system. As Vietnamese is a monosyllabic and tonal language with distinctive linguistic phenomena, text normalization is more complex than in many other languages. Common tasks in this stage include word segmentation, number normalization, abbreviation expansion, handling of special symbols, identification and processing of non-standard words, and conversion of text into appropriate phonetic representations for speech synthesis models [12, 32].

Numerous studies have shown that the quality of text processing directly affects the naturalness and accuracy of synthesized speech, particularly in Vietnamese TTS systems [31]. For instance, incorrect contextual handling of numbers, measurement units, or abbreviations may lead to mispronunciations, thereby reducing the listener’s experience. Therefore, in practical deployments, text processing is often considered one of the most challenging components and requires continuous optimization.
Acoustic Feature Extraction and Representation

After text normalization, the TTS system converts the processed text into intermediate acoustic features. These features typically include mel-spectrograms, pitch, duration, and energy, serving as a bridge between textual input and the final audio waveform. In modern architectures, accurate prediction of these features significantly enhances the modeling of tonal patterns and natural prosody in Vietnamese speech [16, 17].

Several domestic studies have focused on optimizing acoustic feature representations to improve the naturalness and expressiveness of Vietnamese TTS systems [31]. In particular, tonal modeling—an essential characteristic of the Vietnamese language—requires models to capture subtle temporal variations in pitch, as well as the relationship between tonal contours and linguistic context.
Deep Learning Models in TTS

Deep learning models play a central role in the advancement of modern TTS technology. In the early stages, deep neural networks (DNNs) and recurrent architectures such as RNNs and BLSTMs were employed to map textual input to acoustic features. However, these models often faced limitations in capturing long-range contextual dependencies and producing highly natural-sounding speech.

The emergence of sequence-to-sequence architectures with attention mechanisms, notably Tacotron and Tacotron2, marked a significant breakthrough by enabling end-to-end training and more natural speech synthesis [16, 17]. More recently, Transformer-based architectures and parallel models such as FastSpeech and FastPitch have been proposed to improve synthesis speed and stability while maintaining high audio quality [1, 14].
Waveform Generator (Vocoder)

In addition to the acoustic model, the vocoder is the component that determines the final quality of the generated audio signal. Traditional statistical vocoders often produce speech that sounds unnatural and lacks fine-grained details. The emergence of neural vocoders such as WaveNet, WaveGlow, and HiFi-GAN has significantly improved the fidelity and perceptual quality of synthesized speech [17, 23].

Comparative studies indicate that the choice of vocoder has a substantial impact on the perceived quality of Vietnamese TTS systems [23]. In practice, many modern systems combine fast acoustic models with efficient neural vocoders to achieve a balance between speech quality and synthesis speed.
Advanced TTS Technologies and Development Trends

Recently, advanced TTS technologies such as multi-speaker TTS, speaker adaptation, zero-shot TTS, and voice cloning have attracted significant attention from the research community. These approaches enable systems to synthesize multiple voices, adapt to new speakers using only a small amount of data, or even generate speech for speakers not present in the training dataset [13, 18, 26, 27, 40].

For Vietnamese, research on zero-shot and speaker-adaptive TTS is still at an early stage; however, it has demonstrated considerable potential for voice personalization and the expansion of practical TTS applications [13, 27, 40]. These research directions are expected to play an important role in applications such as personalized virtual assistants, intelligent call center systems, and digital education platforms.
Summary of Theoretical Background

Overall, the theoretical foundations and related technologies presented above provide an essential basis for analyzing and evaluating Vietnamese TTS models in the subsequent sections. A clear understanding of each component and core technology not only clarifies the evolutionary trajectory of TTS systems but also facilitates the identification of current limitations and future development opportunities.
Vietnamese Text-to-Speech (TTS) Models
Traditional and Deep Learning–Based TTS Models

Early research on Text-to-Speech (TTS) technology primarily relied on statistical approaches, notably systems based on Hidden Markov Models (HMM) and Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM). These methods were typically designed using a multi-stage architecture, including text preprocessing, acoustic feature extraction, prosody modeling, and waveform synthesis. Although such systems achieved a certain level of stability, traditional TTS approaches often produced speech that sounded mechanical and lacked naturalness, particularly struggling to accurately model the complex tonal system of Vietnamese [12, 31].
Figure 3.1. Typical multi-stage pipeline architecture of a traditional DNN-based TTS system [28].

Figure 3.1 illustrates the typical multi-stage pipeline architecture of traditional TTS systems based on deep neural networks. The input text is first normalized and converted into linguistic features. These features are then fed into duration prediction and DNN-based acoustic models to generate acoustic representations. Finally, a vocoder synthesizes the speech waveform from the predicted acoustic features. Although this architecture improves speech quality compared to purely statistical models, it still relies on a complex processing pipeline and requires substantial manual feature engineering.

To address these limitations, deep learning–based models such as DNNs, RNNs, and BLSTMs were gradually adopted in TTS systems. Deep neural networks enable the learning of nonlinear relationships between textual and acoustic features, significantly enhancing the quality of synthesized speech. Several studies in Vietnam have applied DNN and BLSTM models to Vietnamese TTS systems, demonstrating clear improvements in smoothness and tonal modeling capability compared to traditional approaches [28, 36]. However, these models still depend on a complex pipeline architecture and require multiple manual design steps, posing challenges for scalability and system optimization.
End-to-End TTS Models

The emergence of end-to-end models marked a significant milestone in the field of speech synthesis. Instead of dividing the synthesis process into multiple independent modules, end-to-end architectures learn a direct mapping from input text sequences to intermediate acoustic representations, typically Mel-spectrograms. Tacotron was one of the pioneering architectures in this direction, significantly simplifying the construction of TTS systems [16].

Tacotron2 further improved the original architecture by integrating a Mel-spectrogram generator with a WaveNet-based vocoder, thereby substantially enhancing the quality and naturalness of synthesized speech [17]. Studies applying Tacotron2 to Vietnamese have demonstrated improved tonal modeling capabilities, particularly for long sentences and natural contexts [14, 30, 39]. However, a major limitation of first-generation end-to-end models is their slow inference speed and substantial training data requirements, which pose challenges for real-time applications and deployment on resource-constrained devices.
Figure 3.2. Tacotron architecture for end-to-end speech synthesis [16].

Figure 3.2 illustrates the overall architecture of the Tacotron model, in which input text is encoded at the character level and directly mapped to spectrogram representations through an attention mechanism. The model adopts an encoder–decoder architecture combined with a CBHG module to extract linguistic features, followed by the application of the Griffin–Lim algorithm to reconstruct the speech waveform. Tacotron is considered a major milestone in the development of end-to-end TTS systems, as it eliminates many manual processing steps that were previously required in traditional approaches.
Parallel and Performance-Optimized TTS Models

To address the limitations of traditional end-to-end models in terms of inference speed and stability, several parallel speech synthesis architectures have been proposed. FastSpeech is a representative model that employs a teacher–student distillation mechanism from Tacotron and completely removes the attention mechanism, enabling faster and more stable speech generation.

FastPitch further improves upon FastSpeech by directly incorporating pitch information during training, allowing better prosody control and making it particularly suitable for tonal languages such as Vietnamese [1]. Experimental studies have shown that FastPitch can generate natural and stable speech at significantly higher speeds compared to Tacotron2, while maintaining high audio quality [1, 20]. Therefore, this group of models is considered well-suited for commercial applications and large-scale deployment.
Figure 3.3. FastPitch architecture with pitch and duration prediction [1].

Figure 3.3 illustrates the architecture of FastPitch, a parallel Transformer-based TTS model in which synthesis speed is enhanced through explicit pitch and duration prediction. Unlike autoregressive models, FastPitch generates acoustic features for the entire sequence simultaneously, significantly reducing inference time while maintaining high speech quality. As a result, this model is particularly suitable for Vietnamese TTS applications that require high-speed synthesis and practical deployment capability.
Modern TTS Models and Emerging Trends

In recent years, TTS research has increasingly shifted toward models capable of voice personalization and flexible adaptation under limited data conditions. Multi-speaker TTS systems enable training on data from multiple speakers, allowing a single model to synthesize various voices [15, 33]. Building upon this foundation, speaker adaptation and voice cloning techniques make it possible to generate new voices using only a small amount of target speaker data.
